text: The surgeon opens the peritoneum between the round ligament and the infundibular pelvic ligament on the right side, exposing the external and internal iliac vessels and the ureter. Ureterolysis is performed, dissecting the ureter from the infundibulopelvic ligament to its entry into the bladder. The pararectal space is created, and the uterine artery is identified in the lateral pararectal space. The uterine artery is clipped at the level where it crosses over the ureter, cauterized with bipolar energy, and then cut.

The surgeon creates a window in the posterior leaf of the broad ligament, forming the infundibular pelvic pedicle. The infundibular pelvic ligament is clipped, cauterized, and cut. A similar window is made in the anterior leaf of the broad ligament, where the round ligament is cauterized and cut near the lateral pelvic wall. Dissection of the anterior leaf continues until it reaches the uterovascular fold of the peritoneum. The bladder is dissected and pushed downwards below the upper third of the vagina.

The surgeon repeats the procedure on the left side, opening the left retroperitoneal space to identify the iliac vessels and ureter. The ureter is dissected, and the left pararectal space is created. The left uterine artery is clipped, cauterized, and cut at the crossing over the left ureter. The left infundibulopelvic ligament is similarly clipped, cauterized, and cut. The ureter is dissected and lateralized, and the posterior leaf of the broad ligament is cut and extended to the pouch of Douglas, creating the rectovaginal space and pushing the rectum downwards. The left round ligament is cauterized and cut near the lateral pelvic wall. The anterior cervico-vesical ligament, or ureteric tunnel, is dissected, and the ureter is lateralized.

An angulated endoscopic stapler is used to seal the vaginal cuff before performing a colpotomy. The surgeon carefully protects the bladder and both ureters as the stapler is fired. The distal sealed edge of the vagina is then opened, and the specimen is delivered out vaginally.

The surgeon performs bilateral pelvic lymph node dissection, meticulously removing fibrofatty tissue surrounding the external and internal iliac vessels while maintaining parallel alignment to these vessels.
The surgeon sutures the vaginal vault using 3-0 barbed sutures in a continuous fashion, completing the robotic-assisted type II radical hysterectomy.
